event: Early Neolithic communities of the Starcevo culture settle along the Danube and Morava river valleys.
The Vinca culture flourishes near present-day Belgrade, developing one of Europe's earliest advanced settlements and proto-writing symbols.
Celtic Scordisci tribes establish the settlement of Singidunum at the site of modern Belgrade.
Roman expansion brings the region into the province of Moesia following campaigns against local tribes.
The Roman city of Singidunum becomes a key fortress and home to the Legio IV Flavia Felix.
Emperor Aurelian, born in the region, withdraws Roman troops from Dacia, making the Danube the empire's frontier.
Constantine the Great, born in Naissus (modern Nis), is proclaimed emperor and later legalizes Christianity.
Emperor Justinian I founds Justiniana Prima near his birthplace, reinforcing Byzantine authority in the region.
Slavic tribes, including the ancestors of the Serbs, settle in the Balkans during the reign of Emperor Heraclius.
Frankish chronicles record the Serbs (Sorabi) as a people holding a large part of Dalmatia.
Christianization of the Serbs advances under Byzantine influence and the missions following Saints Cyril and Methodius.
Mihailo of Duklja is recognized as king, marking an early royal title for a Serbian ruler.
Stefan Nemanja becomes Grand Prince of Rascia (Raska), founding the Nemanjic dynasty and the medieval Serbian state.
Stefan the First-Crowned is recognized as King of Serbia, establishing the kingdom's royal legitimacy.
Saint Sava secures autocephaly for the Serbian Orthodox Church and becomes its first archbishop.
Stefan Dusan is crowned Emperor of the Serbs and Greeks, raising Serbia to its greatest territorial extent.
Emperor Dusan promulgates his Code (Dusanov zakonik), a landmark of medieval Serbian law.
The Serbian defeat at the Battle of Maritsa accelerates the empire's fragmentation and Ottoman advance.
Serbian forces under Prince Lazar clash with the Ottomans at the Battle of Kosovo, a defining moment in national memory.
The fall of the fortress of Smederevo completes the Ottoman conquest of medieval Serbia.
Ottoman Sultan Suleiman the Magnificent captures Belgrade, opening the way into Central Europe.
The Patriarchate of Pec is restored, reviving the Serbian Orthodox Church under Ottoman rule.
Patriarch Arsenije III leads the Great Migration of Serbs into Habsburg territory after a failed anti-Ottoman uprising.
The First Serbian Uprising under Karadjordje Petrovic launches the modern Serbian struggle for independence.
The Second Serbian Uprising led by Milos Obrenovic wins de facto autonomy within the Ottoman Empire.
Serbia is recognized as an autonomous principality with Milos Obrenovic as hereditary prince.
The Congress of Berlin recognizes Serbia's full independence from the Ottoman Empire.
Serbia is proclaimed a kingdom with Milan Obrenovic as its first king.
A military coup assassinates King Aleksandar Obrenovic, returning the Karadjordjevic dynasty to the throne under Petar I.
Serbia joins the Balkan League and wins major territory from the Ottomans in the First Balkan War.
The assassination of Archduke Franz Ferdinand in Sarajevo triggers Austria-Hungary's invasion and the start of World War I.
Serbia unites with other South Slav lands to form the Kingdom of Serbs, Croats and Slovenes.
King Aleksandar I renames the state the Kingdom of Yugoslavia and establishes a royal dictatorship.
Axis powers invade and occupy Yugoslavia, and resistance movements including the Partisans emerge in Serbia.
Communist-led Yugoslavia is established as a federal republic with Serbia as a constituent unit under Josip Broz Tito.
The death of Tito unleashes growing political and economic tensions across the Yugoslav federation.
Slobodan Milosevic consolidates power and revokes the autonomy of Kosovo and Vojvodina.
The breakup of Yugoslavia begins as Slovenia and Croatia declare independence, igniting the Yugoslav Wars.
Serbia and Montenegro form the Federal Republic of Yugoslavia amid international sanctions over the Bosnian War.
NATO conducts an air campaign against Yugoslavia over the Kosovo conflict, leading to the withdrawal of Serbian forces from Kosovo.
Mass protests after disputed elections topple Slobodan Milosevic, ending his rule.
The Federal Republic of Yugoslavia is restructured into the State Union of Serbia and Montenegro.
Montenegro's independence referendum leaves Serbia as an independent sovereign state with its own UN seat.
Kosovo declares independence from Serbia, a move Serbia refuses to recognize.
Serbia is granted official candidate status for European Union membership.
Serbia and Kosovo sign the Brussels Agreement to normalize relations under EU mediation.
Serbia formally opens accession negotiations with the European Union.
